does: This paper applies Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a reinforcement learning technique, to improve code-switching speech recognition in audio large language models. The authors identify three specific failure modes in English-Mandarin transcription: language omission (dropping one language), translation-instead-of-transcription (translating rather than transcribing mixed-language content), and hallucination. They construct 100K preference pairs from 570 hours of speech where chosen responses preserve the original language mix while rejected responses simulate these failure patterns. Training three different Audio LLMs with this approach produces consistent behavioral shifts where models learn to transcribe mixed-language content rather than translating it, achieving multilingual error rate reductions up to 89.6% on in-distribution data and 20.0% on out-of-distribution test sets.
connects: This work builds on the established problem of code-switching in speech recognition, which has long challenged even multilingual ASR systems. It extends recent advances in audio language models that demonstrate strong general multilingual capabilities, yet fail systematically on mixed-language inputs. The paper applies DPO, a modern alignment technique developed for large language models, to the audio domain, drawing on prior work showing how preference-based learning can correct specific behavioral failures. This represents a convergence of code-switching research with contemporary alignment methods, responding to the growing capability gap where models possess multilingual knowledge but fail to apply it correctly to code-switched speech.
enables: This work opens directions for using preference optimization to address systematic failures in multimodal and multilingual models more broadly. It suggests that DPO can elicit latent capabilities in foundation models without extensive retraining, potentially applicable to other code-switching language pairs and modalities. The approach could extend to other systematic failures in Audio LLMs or multimodal models where preference pairs reflecting correct behavior can be constructed. Future work might investigate scaling this approach across more language pairs, combining it with other alignment techniques, or applying similar preference-based methods to other domain-specific transcription challenges where models have underlying knowledge but exhibit consistent failure modes.
